revision.text:
[[fr:Jinaicho]][[ja:寺内町]][[simple:Jinaimachi]] [[File:Kyuhoji,_dorui-ato.jpg|right|thumb|220x220px| '''The moat that divided the temple town (Kuboujiji Jinaicho, {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|qid=Q490872|lt=Yao City|1=Yao, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} )''']] '''Jinaicho/Jinaimachi''' (寺内町) refers to self-governing settlements formed around Buddhist temples or dojos (gobo) constructed by the [[Jōdo Shinshū]] sect from the late {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=medieval|1=Middle Ages}} to {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|qid=Q5308718|lt=early modern periods|1=Early modern period}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. These settlements were defensively oriented, surrounded by moats and earthworks, and were populated by believers, merchants, and craftsmen. The name means literally {{Nihongo|Temple|寺|Ji}} {{Nihongo|inside|内|nai}} {{Nihongo|Town|町|Cho/Machi}} The name "Jinaicho" originated because the entire area of the town was considered to be within the precincts of a temple, different from the "monzen-machi" (temple town), which formed outside the temple precincts to serve pilgrims. {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} were self governing communities run by [[Jōdo Shinshū]] in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}.https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/outline/publication/ronbun/ronbun1/pdf/008009.pdf These communities came into existence around temples and gave people protection. They were characterized by the defense functions such as the surrounding moats and earthen walls for fortification, within which Buddhist devotees as well as While some temple towns and cities have declined or disappeared throughout history, towns that have survived to the present day, such as Tondabayashi and Imai, have taken advantage of the Important Preservation District System for Groups of Traditional Buildings, and each temple town has been redeveloped to make use of its historical landscape.merchants and traders lived together.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Jinaicho - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/history/Jinaicho.html|access-date=2024-04-13|website=www.japanesewiki.com|publisher={{ill|National Institute of Information and Communications Technology|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Institute of Information and Communications Technology|qq=}}}} [[File:Isshinden_Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300x300px| Isshindenjimachi, a town formed around Senshu-ji Temple, the head temple of the Jodo Shinshu sect of Buddhism]] == Overview == Jinaicho refers to cities constructed not only by the Jōdo Shinshū sect but also by other Buddhist sects such as Zen, Hokke, and Nichiren during the late medieval to early modern periods.{{Sfn|辻井|2017|qq=draft}} Most Jinaicho were surrounded by moats and comprised religious facilities, residences for religious figures, and townspeople's homes.{{Sfn|辻井|2017}} Notably, Yoshizaki Gobo, Yamashina Honganji, and Osaka Honganji served as strategic bases showing similarities to castle towns of the Warring States period玉井哲雄 「都市の計画と建設」『日本通史』11 近世1、[[朝尾直弘]]ほか（編）、[[:en:岩波書店|岩波書店]]、1993rd year 12月、78頁。, with Yamashina Honganji being regarded as Japan's first fortified city. The defensive attributes of Osaka Honganji, including the construction of a large "yagura" (tower), were particularly notable during its time.[[福島克彦]] 『畿内・近国の戦国合戦』 [[吉川弘文館]]、2009th year 6月、200‐203頁。 The establishment of various Jinaicho generally concentrated around the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, with towns like Tenma in Settsu Province and Yao in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} established with donations from {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} in 1585[[伊藤毅 (建築学者)|伊藤毅]] 「[[Doi:10.3130/aijax.371.0 119|摂津天満本願寺 寺内町の構成(上) : 寺内町の位置,規模,街区構成について]]」『日本建築学会計画系論文報告集』371 日本建築学会、1987th year 、120頁。 and {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} in 1607{{Sfn|辻井|2017}}, respectively. The construction spread widely across advanced regions of Kinai {{Sfn|今井|1973}} but extended over a wide area of the Japanese archipelago, including Hakata in Kyushu and {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen|1=Chikuzen Province}} Province, and the Hokuriku region. Jinaicho often received the right of "kendan" (inspection) from the feudal lords, allowing residents to govern themselves.{{Sfn|今井|1973}} They also gained privileges such as "za" (guild rights), exemption from "kuji" (public duties), "tokusei" (debt relief laws), and exemption from "kuni-shichi" and "sho-shichi" (types of pawn actions)[[仁木宏]] 「寺内町と城下町」『戦国の地域国家』、有光友學（編）、吉川弘文館、2003rd year 5月、275頁。, resulting in lower tax burdens for residents.[[脇田修]] 「[[Doi:10.14989/shirin 41 1|<論説>寺内町の構造と展開]]」『史林』41巻1号 史学研究会 (京都大学文学部内)、1958th year 1月、3‐4頁。 Jinaicho represents one of the most advanced forms of medieval urban integration of religion, economy, and politics, and has been the subject of studies in religious history, folk history, and urban history.[[鍛代敏雄]] 「畿内寺内町と一向一揆」『戦国織豊期の政治と文化』、米原正義先生古稀記念論文集刊行会 （編）、[[続群書類従完成会]]、1993rd year 3月、185頁。 == Cities and Buddhist Temples == Buddhist temples were important in {{ill|History of Japan|en|History of Japan|qid=Q130436|lt=medieval Japanese|1=History of Japan}} cities. They were linked to city growth. Buddhist sects formed in cities. People joined them to escape the [[:en:Caste system|Caste system]]. Priests used to travel. They started staying in one place during rains. Temples were built in easy-to-reach locations. Over time, temples became important city features. "City castles" or tojo made temples more prominent. == Formation and Development of Jinaicho == Jinaicho developed in stages: # Before Jinaicho: Temples were symbols of authority. # Emergence of Jinaicho: Public halls were built for worship. # Central and Regional Jinaicho: There were main and smaller jinaicho. # Transformation of Jinaicho: Jinaicho changed over time. Jodo Shinshu followers led to Jinaicho's rise. They built towns around temples for religious solidarity. == Jinaicho in Urban History == Jinaicho was part of a larger urban trend. In Kyoto, the Hokke sect built a walled area. Sakai and Hirano became fortified cities. This was for defense and independence. Warriors built castle towns, or jokamachi. == List of temple towns == They are common in {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=the Chubu|1=Chūbu region}} and {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kinki regions,|1=Kansai region}} where the influence of the Hongan-ji Temple and the Ikko Ikki was strong. === Chubu region === ==== Toyama Prefecture ==== * Fushiki ( {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|qid=Q132929|lt=Takaoka City|1=Toyama Prefecture}} {{ill|Takaoka, Toyama|en|Takaoka, Toyama|qid=Q502988|lt=, Toyama|1=Takaoka, Toyama}} Prefecture) -Shokoji Temple * Inami ( {{ill|Nanto, Toyama|en|Nanto, Toyama|qid=Q646805|lt=Nanto City,|1=Nanto, Toyama}} Toyama Prefecture) - Zuisenji Temple (the central temple of the Ecchu Ikko Ikki uprising ) * Johana (Nanto City, Toyama Prefecture) -Zentokuji [[善徳寺 (南砺市)|Temple (Nanto City)]] ==== Ishikawa Prefecture ==== * Kanazawa ( {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|qid=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa City,|1=Kanazawa}} {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}} ) - Kanazawa Gobo (the central temple of the Kaga Ikko Ikki uprising ) ==== Fukui Prefecture ==== * Yoshizaki ( {{ill|Awara, Fukui|en|Awara, Fukui|qid=Q538623|lt=Awara City, Fukui|1=Awara, Fukui}} {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|qid=Q133879|lt=Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}} ) - Yoshizaki Gobo ==== Aichi prefecture ==== [[File:本證寺_(安城市)02.jpg|thumb|200x200px| Honshoji Temple (Nodera Head Temple)]] * Nodera ( Nodera-cho, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Anjo|1=Aichi Prefecture}} City, Aichi Prefecture) - Honshoji Temple (Nodera Head Temple). The central temple of the Mikawa Ikko Ikki uprising . * Toro ( {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Fukuoka-cho,|1=Aichi Prefecture}} {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|qid=Q242783|lt=Okazaki City,|1=Okazaki, Aichi}} Aichi Prefecture ) - Former Honshu Temple (Toro Gobo). The central temple of the Mikawa Ikko Ikki uprising . * [[佐々木町|Sasaki]] (Kamisasakicho {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=, Okazaki|1=Aichi Prefecture}} {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|qid=Q242783|lt=City,|1=Okazaki, Aichi}} Aichi Prefecture ) - Former Kamimiyaji Temple (Sasaki Gobo). The core temple of the Mikawa Ikko Ikki uprising . * Harizaki ( {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Harizakicho,|1=Aichi Prefecture}} Okazaki {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|qid=Q242783|lt=City,|1=Okazaki, Aichi}} Aichi Prefecture) - Shomanji Temple (Harizaki Gobo). The core temple of the Mikawa Ikko Ikki uprising . === Kinki === ==== Mie Prefecture ==== * Kawachi ( {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Nagashimacho,|1=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Kuwana, Mie|en|Kuwana, Mie|qid=Q496472|lt=Kuwana City,|1=Kuwana, Mie}} Mie Prefecture ) - Ganshoji Temple . The central temple of the Nagashima Ikko Ikki Uprising . * Isshinden ( {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Tsu City, Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Tsu, Mie|en|Tsu, Mie|qid=Q203027|lt=)|1=Tsu, Mie}} - Senshu-ji Temple (Temple town of the Shinshu Takada sect ) ==== Shiga Prefecture ==== * Kanamori ( Moriyama City, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}} ) - Zenryuji Temple, Kanamori Gobo * Akanoi (Moriyama City, Shiga Prefecture) - Akanoi East Branch Temple, Akanoi West Branch Temple ==== Kyoto ==== * Yamashina ( Yamashina-ku, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City, Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} ) -Yamashina Honganji Temple ==== Osaka Prefecture ==== [[File:Jinaimachi001.jpg|right|thumb|200x200px| Tondabayashi Jinaicho]] * Osaka (Chuo-ku {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=, Osaka City,|1=Osaka Prefecture}} {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} Prefecture ) - Ishiyama Honganji Temple (the central temple of the Ishiyama War ) * Tenma (Kita-ku, Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture ) -Tenma Honganji Temple * Kaneda (Kita-ku {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=, Sakai City,|1=Sakai}} Osaka Prefecture ) - Kounenji Temple, Butsugenji Temple, Chokoji Temple, Saikoji Temple (temple town of the Shinshu Bukkoji sect ) * Tomita ( [[:en:Takatsuki, Osaka|Takatsuki City]], Osaka Prefecture) - Kyogyo -ji Temple * Kaizuka ( Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture) - Gansenji Temple * Hirakata ( {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|qid=Q270912|lt=Hirakata City|1=Hirakata}}, Osaka Prefecture) - Junkoji Temple * Shodai (Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture) - Keio-ji Temple * Exit (Hirakata City, Osaka Prefecture) -Kozenji Temple * Kyuhoji Temple ( {{ill|Yao, Osaka|en|Yao, Osaka|qid=Q490872|lt=Yao City|1=Yao, Osaka}}, Osaka Prefecture) - Kenshoji Temple, Kyuhoji Temple Gobo * Yao (Yao City, Osaka Prefecture) - Daishinji Temple, Yao Gobo * Kayafuri (Yao City, Osaka Prefecture) - Ekoji Temple, Kayafuri Gobo * Tondabayashi ( {{ill|Tondabayashi|en|Tondabayashi|qid=Q490928|lt=Tondabayashi City, Osaka Prefecture|1=Tondabayashi}} ) - Koshoji Tondabayashi Betsuin (Tondabayashi Gobo, a temple town of the Shinshu Koshoha sect ) ( Important Preservation District for Groups of Traditional Buildings ) * Ogatsuka ( {{ill|Kanan, Osaka|en|Kanan, Osaka|qid=Q2305508|lt=Kanan Town,|1=Kanan, Osaka}} Minamikawachi District, Osaka Prefecture) - Kenshoji Temple, Ogatsuka Gobo ==== Hyōgo Prefecture ==== * Amagasaki ( {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|qid=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki City|1=Amagasaki}} {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=, Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} ) - Honkoji Temple and Choenji Temple (temple towns of the Hokke Honmonryu and Nichiren sects, respectively) * Tsukaguchi (Amagasaki City, Hyōgo Prefecture) - Koshoji Tsukaguchi Branch Temple ( Shogenji Temple ) * Obama (Takarazuka City, {{ill|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|en|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|qid=Q823967|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Takarazuka, Hyōgo}} ) - Gosetsuji Temple ==== Nara Prefecture ==== [[File:Imaichō_Kashihara_JPN_002.jpg|right|thumb|200x200px| Imaijinaicho]] * Imai ( {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|qid=Q496319|lt=Kashihara City|1=Kashihara, Nara}} {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=, Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} ) - Shonenji Temple, Imai Gobo (Important Preservation District for Groups of Traditional Buildings) * Takada ( Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture) - Senryu -ji Temple, Takada Gobo * Tawaramoto ( Tawaramoto Town {{ill|Shiki District, Nara|en|Shiki District, Nara|qid=Q1155036|lt=, Ikoma District,|1=Shiki District, Nara}} Nara Prefecture) - Joshoji Temple, Tawaramoto Gobo * Hashio ( Koryo-cho, Kitakatsuragi -gun, Nara {{ill|Kōryō, Nara|en|Kōryō, Nara|qid=Q1346830|lt=Prefecture|1=Kōryō, Nara}} ) - Kyogyo-ji Temple, Hashio Gobo * Higashi-Gose ( Gose City, Nara Prefecture) - Enshoji Temple, Goshogobo * Shimoichi ( Shimoichi Town, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture) - Gangyo- ji Temple, Shimoichi Gobo * Iigai (Yoshino-cho, {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|qid=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino, Nara}} - gun, Nara Prefecture) - Honzenji Temple, Iigai Gobo ==== Wakayama Prefecture ==== * Gobo ( {{ill|Gobō, Wakayama|en|Gobō, Wakayama|qid=Q847572|lt=Gobo City, Wakayama|1=Gobō, Wakayama}} {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} ) - Honganji Temple Hidaka Betsuin == Related articles == * {{ill|Moated settlements|en|Moated settlements|qid=Q60995739|lt=Moated settlement|1=Moated settlements}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=Sogo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * {{ill|Holy city|en|Holy city|qid=Q692581|lt=Holy city|1=Holy city}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|qid=Q1475695|lt=Monk-soldier|1=Sōhei}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|Moated settlements|en|Moated settlements|qid=Q60995739|lt=Enclosed settlements|1=Moated settlements}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺社勢力|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine power|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}/{{ill|10=ja|11=アジール|Asylum|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=asylum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q26377}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|qid=Q1475695|lt=Warrior monks|1=Sōhei}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=Kinai and nearby regions during the Warring States period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} [[File:Isshinden Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300px|Jinaicho formed around the main temple of Jōdo Shinshū Takada sect, Senshūji]] [[File:Kyuhoji, dorui-ato.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Moats that demarcated Jinaicho
(Kyuhoji Jinaicho in Yao City, Osaka Prefecture)]] * == References == {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite journal|last=辻井清吾|date=2017-05|title=信仰から見た「寺内町」構築とその経済的意義|url=http://repo.komazawa-u.ac.jp/opac/repository/all/37736/|journal=佛教経済研究|publisher=駒澤大学仏教経済研究所|volume=46|pages=71-101|ref={{harvid|辻井|2017)}}|和書}}{{Cite journal|last=辻井清吾|date=2017-05|title=信仰から見た「寺内町」構築とその経済的意義|url=http://repo.komazawa-u.ac.jp/opac/repository/all/37736/|journal=佛教経済研究|publisher=駒澤大学仏教経済研究所|volume=46|pages=71-101|ref={{harvid|辻井|2017)}}|和書}} * {{Cite journal|last=今井修平|date=1973-01|title=石山本願寺寺内町に関する一考察|url=https://hdl.handle.net/11094/47958|journal=待兼山論叢. 史学篇|publisher=大阪大学文学部|volume=6|pages=1-18|issn=03874818|ref={{harvid|今井|1973}}|和書}}{{Cite journal|last=今井修平|date=1973-01|title=石山本願寺寺内町に関する一考察|url=https://hdl.handle.net/11094/47958|journal=待兼山論叢. 史学篇|publisher=大阪大学文学部|volume=6|pages=1-18|issn=03874818|ref={{harvid|今井|1973}}|和書}}{{Cite journal|last=今井修平|date=1973-01|title=石山本願寺寺内町に関する一考察|url=https://hdl.handle.net/11094/47958|journal=待兼山論叢. 史学篇|publisher=大阪大学文学部|volume=6|pages=1-18|issn=03874818|ref={{harvid|今井|1973}}|和書}}{{Cite journal|last=今井修平|date=1973-01|title=石山本願寺寺内町に関する一考察|url=https://hdl.handle.net/11094/47958|journal=待兼山論叢. 史学篇|publisher=大阪大学文学部|volume=6|pages=1-18|issn=03874818|ref={{harvid|今井|1973}}|和書}} == Further reading == * {{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Tsujii Seigo |date=May 2017 |url=http://repo.komazawa-u.ac.jp/opac/repository/all/37736/ |title=Economic Significance of Jinaimachi from a Religious Perspective |journal=Buddhist Economic Research |publisher=Komazawa University Institute of Buddhist Economics |volume=46 |pages=71-101 |CRID=1050564288192741376 |ref={{harvid|Tsujii|2017}}}} * {{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Imai Shūhei |date=January 1973 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/11094/47958 |title=A Study on Ishiyama Honganji Jinaimachi |journal=Taikenyama Ronso. History edition |ISSN=03874818 |publisher=Osaka University Faculty of Letters |volume=6 |pages=1-18 |hdl=11094/47958 |CRID=1050001337695439744 |ref={{harvid|Imai|1973}}}} == External link == * [https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_imai/kankou/imaichou/ Streets of Imaicho] * [http://www5d.biglobe.ne.jp/~heritage/ Temple town in Tondabayashi] * [https://www.lake-biwa.net/moriyama-kanegamori/ Biwako River Development Network Kanamori Town] {{Other uses|Term as a historical settlement|The municipality that existed in Minamiakita District, Akita Prefecture|Jinaimachi, Akita}} * [Imai Town Streets](https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_imai/kankou/imaichou/) * [Tondabayashi Jinaicho](http://www5d.biglobe.ne.jp/~heritage/) * [Biwa Lake River Construction Network Kanegamori Town](https://www.lake-biwa.net/moriyama-kanegamori/) {{Urban Planning}} {{Real Estate Development}} {{Architecture of Japan}} {{translated page|ja|寺内町|version=102740073|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:寺内町]] [[fr:Jinaicho]] [[ja:Jinaicho]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho]] {{wikidata link|Q11457718}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Muromachi period]] [[Category:Historical geography]] [[Category:Holy cities]] [[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Non matching pagename on wikidata]] [[Category:Sengoku period]] [[Category:Terms of Jodo Shinsu]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
